resolutions.resolution_evidence: On April 2, 2025, Trump signed Executive Order 14257 declaring a national emergency under IEEPA and imposing a 10% baseline reciprocal tariff on nearly all countries (effective April 5) plus higher country-specific reciprocal rates on ~57 trading partners effective April 9, including EU 20%, Japan 24%, South Korea 25%, China 34% (on top of existing 20%), Vietnam 46%, Taiwan 32%.
resolutions.resolution_derivation: EO 14257 was an official White House action explicitly labeled 'reciprocal tariffs' designed to address trade deficits. It applied to far more than 10 distinct countries (90+) and included at least 3 key European/Asian allies (EU member states, Japan, South Korea, NATO members). The 10% baseline applied to all goods broadly across many HS chapters (not limited to a few industries), satisfying the breadth requirement of Option A. Options B (China-only), C (limited industries), D (negotiations only), E and F do not fit.
resolutions.resolution_weaknesses: Auto, steel, aluminum, semiconductors, pharmaceuticals were excluded from the reciprocal tariffs, which technically narrows the HS chapter coverage — but the breadth still easily exceeds 5 chapters across consumer goods, textiles, machinery, electronics, etc.

resolutions.resolution_evidence: Executive Order 14257 ('Regulating Imports With a Reciprocal Tariff to Rectify Trade Practices That Contribute to Large and Persistent Annual United States Goods Trade Deficits') was signed by President Trump on April 2, 2025. It imposed a 20% reciprocal tariff on imports from the European Union (27 member states), with a 10% baseline applied beginning April 5, 2025 and the higher country-specific rates scheduled for April 9, 2025. This action was formally announced by the White House and covered by the USTR. April 2, 2025 falls between March 26 and May 15, 2025.
resolutions.resolution_derivation: The resolution criterion requires a formal public declaration by USTR, the White House, or Federal Register of 'new tariffs' on EU goods between 2025-03-26 00:00 ET and 2025-05-15 23:59 ET. EO 14257 (April 2, 2025) is a formal White House action imposing duties that were not in effect on March 25, 2025, invoked under IEEPA, and explicitly applies a 20% rate to EU imports. This satisfies the 'new tariffs' definition and the 'official announcement' definition. Resolves YES.
resolutions.resolution_weaknesses: The U.S. Supreme Court later ruled IEEPA-based tariffs invalid, but this post-dates the resolution date (2025-05-16) and does not retroactively unmake the announcement. The question asks about announcement, not validity — so the SCOTUS ruling does not change the resolution.

resolutions.resolution_evidence: On March 12, 2025, the European Commission announced a two-package retaliation strategy in response to US Section 232 steel and aluminum tariffs. The countermeasures covered approximately €18 billion (~$19B USD) of US-origin goods and included reimposition of the 2018/2020 retaliatory tariffs (which targeted products such as bourbon whiskey at 25%, motorcycles at 25%, jeans, boats, and other products at rates at or above 25%) plus an additional package of retaliatory tariffs. First countermeasures were scheduled for April 15, 2025 with the remainder on May 15, 2025.
resolutions.resolution_derivation: Option A requires new tariffs of 20%+ on over $10B of US goods. EU retaliation covered €18B (~$19B) — above $10B threshold. The reimposed 2018/2020 lists contained multiple product categories at 25% or higher (bourbon, motorcycles, jeans, denim). Announcement was made March 12, 2025, with April 15 implementation, both dates within the April 15, 2025 resolution window. Per resolution criteria prioritization (A > B > C > D > E), Option A governs because tariffs above the 20% threshold and $10B threshold were announced. Resolves OPTION A.
resolutions.resolution_weaknesses: One subtle issue: Option A says 'over $10B of US goods' — if the €18B figure refers to the *combined* March + May packages and the specifically-announced-by-April-15 tranche was smaller, the threshold may not be cleanly met by April 15 alone. Also, the weighted-average tariff rate across the €18B basket could be below 20% even though specific items are at 25%+ — the criterion wording is ambiguous on whether 'tariffs of 20% or more' applies to the package average or to any item in the package. Pass B should verify.

resolutions.resolution_evidence: On March 6, 2025, Trump issued executive orders delaying the 25% IEEPA tariffs on Canada and Mexico for USMCA-qualifying goods (the vast majority of bilateral trade). The April 2 'Liberation Day' EO 14257 explicitly exempted Canada and Mexico from the new reciprocal tariff regime, leaving the prior carve-out structure in place. As of April 15, 2025, only non-USMCA-compliant goods faced the 25% tariff (10% for Canadian energy/potash); USMCA-compliant goods remained exempt.
resolutions.resolution_derivation: USMCA-compliant goods constitute well over 50% of US imports from Canada and Mexico by 2024 value (estimates ~62-95% depending on category). Therefore on April 15, 2025, new general tariffs were in effect on less than 50% of the initially targeted goods by value, satisfying the second clause of Option C. Option A (25%+ on majority) fails because majority is exempt; Option B fails for same reason; Option D fails because tariffs were not formally canceled.
resolutions.resolution_weaknesses: Estimates of the USMCA-compliant share vary by source and methodology; if a strict reading of '2024 import data from US Census Bureau' on the precise originally-targeted product list yielded a USMCA-compliant share below 50%, Option A could apply instead.

resolutions.resolution_evidence: On March 3, 2025, President Trump invoked IEEPA to implement 25% tariffs on goods imported from Canada and Mexico, effective 12:01 a.m. ET March 4, 2025 (energy and energy resources from Canada subject to a 10% rate). The action was executed via executive order and published in the Federal Register. U.S. imports from Canada totaled roughly $420B in 2024 (well above the $50B annual threshold), and the 25% rate applied broadly to non-energy goods.
resolutions.resolution_derivation: Criterion requires: (1) official executive-branch action (EO/proclamation/Federal Register notice); (2) new tariffs of 25%+; (3) annual value ≥$50B; (4) before April 1, 2025. March 4, 2025 implementation of the 25% IEEPA tariff on Canadian goods satisfies all four. Energy carve-out at 10% does not defeat the threshold because non-energy Canadian imports alone far exceed $50B annually.
resolutions.resolution_weaknesses: A strict reading might ask whether the 10% energy carve-out reduces the scope to below $50B of 25%+ tariffed goods. Canadian non-energy imports to the US substantially exceed $50B — petroleum & gas were roughly a quarter of 2024 Canadian exports to US, leaving well over $300B subject to the 25% rate. So even under the strict reading, YES holds.

resolutions.resolution_evidence: Between April 3 and June 15, 2025, USTR did not publish any Federal Register notice that explicitly lowered the ad valorem duty rate on a Section 301 product from China. The only USTR Section 301 actions in this window were (a) the April 17/23 maritime/shipbuilding Section 301 notice (an action expanding tariffs), (b) the May 31, 2025 product exclusion extension (90 FR 23987, June 5) which extended existing exclusions through Aug 31 but did not lower the underlying duty rate, and (c) the June 6 proposed modification of maritime Section 301. The May 12 US-China Geneva agreement reduced reciprocal IEEPA tariffs (not Section 301) via Presidential EO, not a USTR Federal Register notice.
resolutions.resolution_derivation: The fine print explicitly states tariff exclusions that exempt products without changing the underlying tariff rate do NOT count. The Geneva-related reduction (125% to 10%) was on the IEEPA reciprocal tariff (Section 301 tariffs were explicitly preserved per White & Case and Akerman analyses), and was implemented by Presidential proclamation rather than a USTR Federal Register notice. Therefore no qualifying USTR notice exists in the resolution window.
resolutions.resolution_weaknesses: If a niche USTR notice during this window happened to lower a specific Section 301 ad valorem rate (e.g., a technical correction notice not surfaced in major coverage), the resolution could flip to YES — but no such notice appears in available sources.

resolutions.resolution_derivation: Per the criteria, the Section 301 maritime/shipbuilding action expanded Section 301 tariffs to new product categories (port fees, ship-to-shore cranes) not previously subject to Section 301 — fitting Option D ('expand to new products'). This was the first significant Section 301-specific action in the window. Option A (formal negotiations + pause) fails since negotiations were on broader IEEPA reciprocal tariffs and Section 301 actions continued to expand. Option B (rate reduction or product-wide exclusions) fails — the May 31 extension was of existing narrow exclusions, not new product-wide exclusions. Option C (company-specific expansion only) fails. Option E fails.
resolutions.resolution_weaknesses: If interpreting 'first and most significant' to require Section 301-specific action only, the May 12 Geneva pause (which preserved Section 301 tariffs) muddies the picture; additionally, the maritime Section 301 was technically a separate Section 301 case rather than a modification of the original China IP Section 301 tariffs.

resolutions.resolution_evidence: On April 8 and April 9, 2025, Trump issued executive orders increasing the reciprocal IEEPA tariff on imports from China to 84% then 125% (on top of the 20% fentanyl tariff = 145%), explicitly targeting all goods originating from China. Although automobiles were excluded from the reciprocal tariff list, the March 26 Section 232 auto proclamation imposed a new 25% tariff on imported autos including EVs, which stacks on top of the existing 100% Section 301 EV rate, effectively raising the total tariff on Chinese EVs above 100% (to 125%+). Multiple official White House announcements explicitly target Chinese imports during the window.
resolutions.resolution_derivation: The criteria allow YES if there is either (a) an explicit increase above the existing 100% rate on Chinese EVs OR (b) a new tariff on a specific category of EVs/EV components from China that did not previously exist. The April 8/9 reciprocal tariff EOs explicitly target imports from China and apply to Chinese-origin EV components/batteries that were not on the auto exemption list, which had not previously been subject to specific tariffs beyond MFN. Furthermore, USTR's May 2024 Section 301 four-year-review actions (already in effect April 4, 2025) had set the 100% on Chinese EVs, and additional Section 301 maritime action included Chinese ship-to-shore cranes and stacking of new tariffs. The unambiguous IEEPA reciprocal tariffs of 84%/125% are official US government announcements on or before July 1, 2025, that are explicitly China-specific and apply to imports including EV-related goods.
resolutions.resolution_weaknesses: A strict reading requires the announcement to specifically name 'electric vehicles' or define an EV-specific category — the April reciprocal tariff EOs target China broadly, not EVs by name, and autos were excluded from the reciprocal tariff. If interpreted strictly, no announcement explicitly named 'EVs from China' as the target during this window, which would push the resolution to NO.

resolutions.resolution_derivation: The Section 232 investigation is a formal, comprehensive supply chain review/inter-agency study specifically focused on the semiconductor industry, with no simultaneous announcement of new tariffs or new export controls in the window. This satisfies Option D's criteria (formal supply chain review or inter-agency study without implementing new tariffs or controls). Options A (broad 15%+ tariffs), B (China-targeted tariffs), C (new export controls without tariffs) all fail since no such actions were announced in the window. Option E fails because the Section 232 investigation is a significant new executive action.

The report was only transmitted privately to the President on June 30, 2025 (still after the deadline), and was not made public until subsequent Presidential action in late July/early August 2025. A Federal Register notice of public comment does not contain a preliminary finding per the criteria.

resolutions.resolution_evidence: On April 7, 2025, the Supreme Court (Trump v. J.G.G., 24A931) vacated Judge Boasberg's TRO on procedural (venue) grounds but required notice and hearing. After brief flight activity, on April 19, 2025, SCOTUS issued an emergency injunction in A.A.R.P. v. Trump (24A1007) pausing AEA removals of detainees in northern Texas. Per Solicitor General Sauer's filing, 'The government has agreed not to remove pursuant to the AEA those AEA detainees who do file habeas claims,' and the administration complied with the pause while pursuing litigation. By May 15, 2025, no new executive order or modified AEA policy was issued, and no public announcement was made resuming deportations in defiance of the courts.
resolutions.resolution_derivation: Option A fails: by May 15 the administration did not publicly announce defiance of the injunction and resume flights without a stay — it was complying with the April 19 SCOTUS pause. Option C fails: no rescission of the March 14 AEA proclamation or new narrower executive order/Federal Register publication occurred by May 15. Option D fails: the administration did take significant action — filing emergency briefs at SCOTUS, complying with the injunction, and continuing the appeal — which the criteria for Option B describe ('continues to fully comply with the existing judicial injunction... while its emergency application for a stay is pending'). The SCOTUS had not issued a final adverse ruling by May 15 (the 7-2 ruling clarifying notice requirements came May 16, after the resolution date). Option B best matches.
resolutions.resolution_weaknesses: The fact pattern doesn't perfectly match Option B's framing — the original 'judicial blockade' (Boasberg's TRO) was actually vacated by SCOTUS on April 7 (granting the administration's stay), and the May 15 status involves a different injunction (April 19 SCOTUS pause for Texas detainees). A strict reading could classify this as 'no longer the same emergency application' or even Option D since the original blockade was lifted, but Option B remains the closest substantive match to 'comply with injunction + appeal pending.'
